free_text: Angular Distribution of Fragments from Fission
Induced by Heavy Ions in Gold and Bismuth
The Berkeley Hilac.
Unsupported targets of gold and bismuth ranging in
thickness from about 500 to 1000 microg/cm2 were used.
The fission-fragment activities were measured
simultaneously at 32 counting stations equipped with
Geiger-Muller tubes. The register pulse from each
counter was modified so that it would feed into a
specific channel of a Penco 100-channel pulse-height
analyzer. Counting was continued long enough to show
that the angular distribution determined by gross
beta counting did not depend on the time after the
end of bombardment. The measurements corresponding to
the bombardments with N14 and 016 have been checked
further in experiments using solid-state detectors.
BP
Errors due to counting statistics,
differences in counter geometries, and any induced
activation in the catchers. The errors represent
standard deviations.
